skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X workflows with inference.sh CLI commands for posts, media posts, likes, retweets, DMs, follows, deletes, and profile lookup.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X posting and engagement actions through guided infsh CLI commands.
skill.author: skillssh

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are true positives because the skill authorizes Bash execution of infsh commands for authenticated Twitter/X actions. Network URL findings are expected for this integration, and the weak cryptography findings at SKILL.md lines 3 and 27 appear to be false positives with no crypto use found. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Twitter/X Actions Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash(infsh *) and provides infsh commands that post, like, retweet, delete, DM, follow, and read Twitter/X data. This is legitimate automation, but it can change an authenticated social account and publish content externally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool and examples directly invoke infsh for account-changing Twitter/X operations. The behavior is intentional and documented, but it requires user trust and account authorization.

medium_findings.title: Social Automation Abuse Potential
medium_findings.description: The skill includes engagement actions such as DMs, following users, liking posts, and retweeting. These actions can support legitimate workflows but also create spam, harassment, or policy violation risk if used without controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed app IDs include DMs, follows, likes, retweets, and deletes. These are high-impact social account actions even though no malicious intent is shown.

medium_findings.title: Shell Quoting Risk With User-Supplied Content
medium_findings.description: Several examples pass tweet and DM text as single-quoted JSON directly to Bash. If generated user text is inserted without escaping or a file input, shell quoting mistakes could alter the command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are examples, not executable code that automatically concatenates input. The risk depends on whether an agent inserts unescaped user content into Bash.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Integration References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, install instructions, an image, and an example media URL placeholder. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or hidden account data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and media references in Markdown. I did not find a network exfiltration pattern or credential transmission to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static blocker matches at SKILL.md lines 3 and 27 do not show cryptographic algorithms or cryptographic code. They appear to match ordinary text in the description and app table.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is the skill description, and line 27 is a Markdown table header. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Allowlist For infsh Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands. This pattern can run external commands, reach external services, and modify authenticated Twitter/X account state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Bash allowlist and examples directly show external CLI execution. The commands are central to the skill rather than incidental references.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Dependent Social Posting Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on inference.sh and Twitter/X integrations for posting, media, engagement, and account lookups. This requires external network access and authenticated third-party services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents inference.sh and Twitter/X integration URLs. Network use is expected and required for the advertised behavior.

content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X posts and engagement by hand can slow campaigns and create inconsistent responses. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through infsh commands for posting, DMs, follows, and profile lookups.
actual_capabilities: Post text tweets through x/post-tweet using infsh.
Create posts with media URLs through x/post-create.
Like, retweet, delete, and read posts by tweet ID.
Send DMs to recipient IDs and follow users by username.
Retrieve Twitter/X user profiles through x/user-get.
Use generated image or video outputs as media post inputs.
limitations: Requires the infsh CLI and an authenticated Twitter/X integration.
Does not include moderation, approval routing, or policy checks.
Can perform public account actions through external services.
Media posts depend on reachable media URLs or app outputs.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Prepare and post text or media updates from an authenticated Twitter/X account.

use_cases.title: Handle Routine Engagement
use_cases.description: Like, retweet, follow, or send approved DMs from repeatable prompts.

use_cases.title: Inspect Account And Post Data
use_cases.description: Retrieve user profiles or post details before deciding the next action.

prompt_templates.title: Post A Basic Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Post this approved message to Twitter/X: [message]. Show me the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Media Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Twitter/X post with this message and media URL: [message], [media URL]. Confirm the final text first.

prompt_templates.title: Review And Engage
prompt_templates.prompt: Get details for this post ID, summarize it, and ask before liking or retweeting it: [post ID].

prompt_templates.title: Build A Generated Media Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate media from this creative brief, prepare a Twitter/X post, and request approval before publishing: [brief].

output_examples.output: The assistant drafts the tweet text, shows the planned infsh action, and asks for confirmation before publishing.

output_examples.input: Share a product image with a short caption.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a media post using the supplied image URL and confirms the final caption.

output_examples.input: Check a user profile before sending a DM.
output_examples.output: The assistant retrieves the profile information and presents a short summary before any message is sent.

best_practices: Review every tweet, DM, follow, like, and retweet before execution.
Use saved input files for long text to reduce shell quoting errors.
Keep Twitter/X credentials and infsh sessions limited to trusted workspaces.
anti_patterns: Do not run bulk follows, DMs, likes, or retweets without approval.
Do not paste secrets, tokens, or private customer data into post text.
Do not publish generated content without brand and policy review.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: Yes. It can run infsh commands that post through an authenticated Twitter/X integration.

faq.question: Does it require a separate CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires the inference.sh CLI, also called infsh.

faq.question: Can it post media?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports media posts when a valid media URL or generated media output is available.

faq.question: Can it send direct messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an x/dm-send app for messages to recipient IDs.

faq.question: Is it safe for unattended automation?
faq.answer: No. Account-changing actions should require review and explicit approval.

faq.question: What tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
